A **scientist** is an expert who conducts [scientific research](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method") to advance knowledge in [science](/wiki/Science "Science").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-researchcouncil-2)

In [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), there was no real ancient analog of a modern scientist. Instead, [philosophers](/wiki/Philosopher "Philosopher") engaged in the philosophical study of nature called [natural philosophy](/wiki/Natural_philosophy "Natural philosophy").[[3]](#cite_note-Lehoux-3) Though [Thales](/wiki/Thales "Thales") (c. 624–545 BC) was arguably the first scientist for describing how cosmic events may be seen as natural, not necessarily caused by [gods](/wiki/Deity "Deity"),[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-CPM-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-books.google.com-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) it was not until the [19th century](/wiki/19th_century_in_science "19th century in science") that the term *scientist* came into regular use: it was coined by the [theologian](/wiki/Theologian "Theologian"), [philosopher](/wiki/Philosopher "Philosopher"), and historian of science [William Whewell](/wiki/William_Whewell "William Whewell") in 1833 to describe [Mary Somerville](/wiki/Mary_Somerville "Mary Somerville").[[10]](#cite_note-Cahan_Natural_Philosophy-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Lightman2011-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Aristotle_Altemps_Inv8575.jpg/250px-Aristotle_Altemps_Inv8575.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aristotle_Altemps_Inv8575.jpg)

Ferngren[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Georgius_Agricola.jpg/250px-Georgius_Agricola.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgius_Agricola.jpg)

Generally referred to as the father of [mineralogy](/wiki/Mineralogy "Mineralogy") and the founder of [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology") as a scientific discipline.[[13]](#cite_note-berk-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Rafferty_2012_p._10-14)

The roles of "scientists", and their predecessors before the emergence of modern scientific disciplines, have evolved considerably over time. Scientists of different eras (and before them, natural philosophers, mathematicians, natural historians, natural theologians, engineers, and others who contributed to the development of science) have had widely different places in society, and the [social norms](/wiki/Social_norms "Social norms"), [ethical values](/wiki/Ethical_values "Ethical values"), and [epistemic virtues](/wiki/Epistemic_virtues "Epistemic virtues") associated with scientists—and expected of them—have changed over time as well. Accordingly, many different historical figures can be identified as early scientists, depending on which characteristics of modern science are taken to be essential.

Some historians point to the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") that began in 16th century as the period when science in a recognizably modern form developed. It was not until the 19th century that sufficient socioeconomic changes had occurred for scientists to emerge as a major profession.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Classical antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classical antiquity")]

[Knowledge about nature in classical antiquity](/wiki/History_of_science_in_classical_antiquity "History of science in classical antiquity") was pursued by many kinds of scholars. [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") contributions to science—including works of geometry and mathematical astronomy, early accounts of biological processes and catalogs of plants and animals, and theories of knowledge and learning—were produced by [philosophers](/wiki/Philosopher "Philosopher") and [physicians](/wiki/Physician "Physician"), as well as practitioners of various trades. These roles, and their associations with scientific knowledge, spread with the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and, with the [spread of Christianity](/wiki/History_of_Christianity#Roman_Empire "History of Christianity"), became closely linked to religious institutions in most European countries. [Astrology](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology") and [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") became an important area of knowledge, and the role of astronomer/astrologer developed with the support of political and religious [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage"). By the time of the [medieval university](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university") system, knowledge was divided into the *[trivium](/wiki/Trivium_(education) "Trivium (education)")*—philosophy, including [natural philosophy](/wiki/Natural_philosophy "Natural philosophy")—and the *[quadrivium](/wiki/Quadrivium "Quadrivium")*—mathematics, including astronomy. Hence, the medieval analogs of scientists were often either philosophers or mathematicians. Knowledge of plants and animals was broadly the province of physicians.

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[Science in medieval Islam](/wiki/Science_in_medieval_Islam "Science in medieval Islam") developed new approaches to acquiring natural knowledge, although these developments remained within existing social roles such as philosopher and mathematician. Many proto-scientists of the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age") are considered [polymaths](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath"), partly because there were no clearly defined [scientific disciplines](/wiki/Scientific_discipline "Scientific discipline") as understood today.

Several of these early polymaths were also religious scholars. For example, [Alhazen](/wiki/Alhazen "Alhazen") and [al-Biruni](/wiki/Ab%C5%AB_Rayh%C4%81n_al-B%C4%ABr%C5%ABn%C4%AB "Abū Rayhān al-Bīrūnī") were associated with [mutakallimiin](/wiki/Kalam "Kalam"); the physician [Avicenna](/wiki/Avicenna "Avicenna") was a [hafiz](/wiki/Hafiz_(Quran) "Hafiz (Quran)"); the physician [Ibn al-Nafis](/wiki/Ibn_al-Nafis "Ibn al-Nafis") was a hafiz, [muhaddith](/wiki/Muhaddith "Muhaddith"), and [ulema](/wiki/Ulema "Ulema"); the botanist [Otto Brunfels](/wiki/Otto_Brunfels "Otto Brunfels") was a theologian and historian of Protestantism; and the astronomer and physician [Nicolaus Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus") was a cleric.

During the [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance"), figures such as [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"), [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei"), and [Gerolamo Cardano](/wiki/Gerolamo_Cardano "Gerolamo Cardano") are often regarded as notable polymaths.

### Renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Renaissance")]

During the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), Italian scholars made significant contributions to science. [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci") made notable observations in paleontology and anatomy. [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei"), sometimes referred to as the father of modern science,[[18]](#cite_note-father_of_science_Einstein-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) improved the thermometer and telescope, enabling more detailed observations of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System").

[Descartes](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Descartes "René Descartes") pioneered [analytic geometry](/wiki/Analytic_geometry "Analytic geometry"), formulated a [theory](/wiki/Theory "Theory") of [mechanics](/wiki/Mechanics "Mechanics"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) and proposed ideas concerning [animal movement](/wiki/Muscle_contraction "Muscle contraction") and [perception](/wiki/Perception "Perception").

Research into [vision](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception") engaged physicists such as [Thomas Young](/wiki/Thomas_Young_(scientist) "Thomas Young (scientist)") and [Hermann von Helmholtz](/wiki/Hermann_von_Helmholtz "Hermann von Helmholtz"), who also studied [optics](/wiki/Optics "Optics"), [hearing](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing"), and [music](/wiki/Music "Music"). [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") expanded upon earlier mathematical developments by co-inventing [calculus](/wiki/Calculus "Calculus") (independently of [Leibniz](/wiki/Gottfried_Leibniz "Gottfried Leibniz")). He formulated the principles of [classical mechanics](/wiki/Classical_mechanics "Classical mechanics") and conducted extensive investigations into [light](/wiki/Light "Light") and optics.

[Joseph Fourier](/wiki/Joseph_Fourier "Joseph Fourier") developed the theory of [infinite periodic series](/wiki/Fourier_series "Fourier series"), studied [heat](/wiki/Heat "Heat") transfer and [infrared radiation](/wiki/Infrared_radiation "Infrared radiation"), and described what later became known as the [greenhouse effect](/wiki/Greenhouse_effect "Greenhouse effect"). Mathematicians including [Girolamo Cardano](/wiki/Girolamo_Cardano "Girolamo Cardano"), [Blaise Pascal](/wiki/Blaise_Pascal "Blaise Pascal"), [Pierre de Fermat](/wiki/Pierre_de_Fermat "Pierre de Fermat"), [John von Neumann](/wiki/John_von_Neumann "John von Neumann"), [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing"), [Aleksandr Khinchin](/wiki/Aleksandr_Khinchin "Aleksandr Khinchin"), [Andrey Markov](/wiki/Andrey_Markov "Andrey Markov"), and [Norbert Wiener](/wiki/Norbert_Wiener "Norbert Wiener") made major contributions to mathematics and [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), including foundational work relevant to [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), [statistical mechanics](/wiki/Statistical_mechanics "Statistical mechanics"), and [quantum mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"). Several mathematically inclined scientists, including Galileo, were also accomplished [musicians](/wiki/Musician "Musician").

Developments in [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine") and [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology") included advances in understanding the circulation of [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood"), from [Galen](/wiki/Galen "Galen") to [Harvey](/wiki/William_Harvey "William Harvey"). Some scholars and historians have argued that [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") contributed to the rise of the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Gilley1-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Age of Enlightenment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Age of Enlightenment")]

During the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), [Luigi Galvani](/wiki/Luigi_Galvani "Luigi Galvani"), a pioneer of [bioelectromagnetics](/wiki/Bioelectromagnetics "Bioelectromagnetics"), investigated what he termed "animal electricity." He observed that applying an electrical charge to the spinal cord of a frog could produce muscular spasms throughout its body. Even detached frog legs were seen to twitch when exposed to electrical stimulation. In one experiment, Galvani noted that a steel scalpel touching a brass hook holding a frog’s leg caused the leg to contract.

Further experiments reinforced these observations, leading Galvani to conclude that he was witnessing a form of intrinsic electrical force within animal tissue. At the [University of Pavia](/wiki/University_of_Pavia "University of Pavia"), his colleague [Alessandro Volta](/wiki/Alessandro_Volta "Alessandro Volta") replicated the results but questioned Galvani's interpretation.[[26]](#cite_note-Routledge-26)

[Lazzaro Spallanzani](/wiki/Lazzaro_Spallanzani "Lazzaro Spallanzani") was a prominent figure in experimental physiology and the natural sciences. His investigations had a lasting influence on medical science, particularly in the experimental study of bodily functions and animal reproduction.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[Francesco Redi](/wiki/Francesco_Redi "Francesco Redi") demonstrated that [microorganisms](/wiki/Microorganism "Microorganism") could cause [disease](/wiki/Disease "Disease").

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 19th century")]

Until the late 19th or early 20th century, scientists were commonly referred to as "[natural philosophers](/wiki/Natural_philosophy "Natural philosophy")" or "men of science".[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

English philosopher and historian of science [William Whewell](/wiki/William_Whewell "William Whewell") coined the term *scientist* in 1833. It first appeared in print in his anonymous 1834 review of [Mary Somerville](/wiki/Mary_Somerville "Mary Somerville")'s *[On the Connexion of the Physical Sciences](/wiki/On_the_Connexion_of_the_Physical_Sciences "On the Connexion of the Physical Sciences")*, published in the *[Quarterly Review](/wiki/Quarterly_Review "Quarterly Review")*.[[32]](#cite_note-Ross1962-32)

In the review, Whewell discussed what he described as an increasing tendency toward specialization within the sciences. As highly specific terms such as *chemist*, *mathematician*, and *naturalist* became common, the broader term *philosopher* no longer adequately described those engaged in scientific study. Whewell contrasted this trend with Somerville's aim of demonstrating how distinct branches of science had historically been unified through general principles.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Whewell reported that members of the [British Association for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/British_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "British Association for the Advancement of Science") had expressed concern over the absence of a suitable collective term for "students of the knowledge of the material world." Referring indirectly to himself, he noted that "some ingenious gentleman" had proposed the word *scientist* by analogy with *artist*, arguing that similar formations such as *[economist](/wiki/Economist "Economist")* and *[atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist")* were already in use. The suggestion, however, was not immediately well received.[[34]](#cite_note-Holmes-34)

Whewell later proposed the term again, more explicitly, in his 1840 work *The Philosophy of the Inductive Sciences*.[[35]](#cite_note-Whewell-35)

> The terminations *ize* (rather than *ise*), *ism*, and *ist*, are applied to words of all origins: thus we have to *pulverize*, to *colonize*, *Witticism*, *Heathenism*, *Journalist*, *Tobacconist*. Hence we may make such words when they are wanted. As we cannot use *physician* for a cultivator of physics, I have called him a *Physicist*. We need very much a name to describe a cultivator of science in general. I should incline to call him a *Scientist*. Thus we might say, that as an Artist is a Musician, Painter, or Poet, a Scientist is a Mathematician, Physicist, or Naturalist.

He also proposed the term *physicist* as a counterpart to the French *physicien*. Neither term gained widespread acceptance immediately. *Scientist* became common in the late 19th century in the United States and around the turn of the 20th century in [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain").[[32]](#cite_note-Ross1962-32)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

By the twentieth century, the modern concept of science as a distinct body of knowledge, practiced by a specialized community and pursued through recognized methods, had become firmly established.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[Marie Curie](/wiki/Marie_Curie "Marie Curie") became the first woman to win the Nobel Prize and the first person to win it twice. Her efforts led to the development of nuclear energy and Radiotherapy for the treatment of cancer. In 1922, she was appointed a member of the International Commission on Intellectual Co-operation by the Council of the League of Nations. She campaigned for scientist's right to patent their discoveries and inventions. She also campaigned for free access to international scientific literature and for internationally recognized scientific symbols.

## Profession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Profession")]

As a profession, the scientist of today is widely recognized[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. However, there is no formal process to determine who is a scientist and who is not a scientist. Anyone can be a scientist in some sense. Some professions have legal requirements for their practice (e.g. [licensure](/wiki/Licensure "Licensure")) and some scientists are [independent scientists](/wiki/Independent_scientist "Independent scientist") meaning that they practice science on their own, but to practice science there are no known [licensure](/wiki/Licensure "Licensure") requirements.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Education")]

In modern times, many professional scientists are trained in an [academic setting](/wiki/Academy "Academy") (e.g., [universities](/wiki/Universities "Universities") and [research institutes](/wiki/Research_institute "Research institute")), mostly at the level of [graduate schools](/wiki/Graduate_school "Graduate school"). Upon completion, they would normally attain an [academic degree](/wiki/Academic_degree "Academic degree"), with the highest degree being a [doctorate](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") such as a [Doctor of Philosophy](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") (PhD).[[39]](#cite_note-Cyranoski2011-39) Although [graduate education](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education") for scientists varies among institutions and countries, some common training requirements include [specializing](/wiki/Academic_specialization "Academic specialization") in an [area](/wiki/Branches_of_science "Branches of science") of interest,[[40]](#cite_note-Editorial2015-40) publishing research findings in [peer-reviewed](/wiki/Peer_review "Peer review") [scientific journals](/wiki/Scientific_journal "Scientific journal")[[41]](#cite_note-Gould2016-41) and presenting them at [scientific conferences](/wiki/Academic_conference "Academic conference"),[[42]](#cite_note-Kruger2018-42) giving [lectures](/wiki/Lecture "Lecture") or [teaching](/wiki/Teacher "Teacher"),[[42]](#cite_note-Kruger2018-42) and defending a [thesis (or dissertation)](/wiki/Thesis "Thesis") during an [oral examination](/wiki/Oral_exam "Oral exam").[[39]](#cite_note-Cyranoski2011-39) To aid them in this endeavor, graduate students often work under the guidance of a [mentor](/wiki/Mentorship "Mentorship"), usually a senior scientist, which may continue after the completion of their doctorates whereby they work as [postdoctoral researchers](/wiki/Postdoctoral_researcher "Postdoctoral researcher").[[43]](#cite_note-Lee2007-43)

### Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Career")]

After the completion of their training, many scientists pursue careers in a variety of work settings and conditions.[[44]](#cite_note-Kwok2017-44) In 2017, the British [scientific journal](/wiki/Scientific_journal "Scientific journal") *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")* published the results of a large-scale survey of more than 5,700 doctoral students worldwide, asking them which [sectors of the economy](/wiki/Economic_sector "Economic sector") they would like to work in. A little over half of the respondents wanted to pursue a career in academia, with smaller proportions hoping to work in industry, government, and nonprofit environments.[[45]](#cite_note-Editorial2017a-45)[[46]](#cite_note-Woolston2017-46)

Other motivations are recognition by their peers and prestige. The [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize"), a widely regarded prestigious award,[[47]](#cite_note-Stockton2014-47) is awarded annually to those who have achieved scientific advances in the fields of [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), and [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry").

Some scientists have a desire to apply scientific knowledge for the benefit of people's health, the nations, the world, nature, or industries (academic scientist and [industrial scientist](/wiki/Industrial_scientist "Industrial scientist")). Scientists tend to be less motivated by direct financial reward for their work than other careers. As a result, scientific researchers often accept lower average salaries when compared with many other professions which require a similar amount of training and qualification.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Research interests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Research interests")]

Scientists include [experimentalists](/wiki/Experiment "Experiment") who mainly perform experiments to test hypotheses, and [theoreticians](/wiki/Scientific_theory "Scientific theory") who mainly develop [models](/wiki/Scientific_modelling "Scientific modelling") to explain existing data and predict new results. There is a continuum between the two activities and the division between them is not clear-cut, with many scientists performing both tasks.

Those considering science as a [career](/wiki/Career "Career") often look to the frontiers. These include [cosmology](/wiki/Physical_cosmology "Physical cosmology") and [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"), especially [molecular biology](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology") and the [human genome](/wiki/Human_genome "Human genome") project. Other areas of active research include the exploration of [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") at the scale of [elementary particles](/wiki/Elementary_particle "Elementary particle") as described by [high-energy physics](/wiki/Particle_physics "Particle physics"), and [materials science](/wiki/Materials_science "Materials science"), which seeks to discover and design new materials. Others choose to study [brain](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain") function and [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter"), which is considered by many to be the "final frontier".[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) There are many important discoveries to make regarding the nature of the [mind](/wiki/Mind "Mind") and [human](/wiki/Human "Human") [thought](/wiki/Thought "Thought"), much of which still remains unknown.

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: United States")]

According to the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), 4.7 million people with science degrees worked in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") in 2015, across all disciplines and employment sectors. The figure included twice as many men as women. Of that total, 17% worked in academia, that is, at universities and undergraduate institutions, and men held 53% of those positions. 5% of scientists worked for the federal government, and about 3.5% were self-employed. Of the latter two groups, two-thirds were men. 59% of scientists in the United States were employed in industry or business, and another 6% worked in non-profit positions.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### By gender

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientist&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: By gender")]

See also: [Women in science](/wiki/Women_in_science "Women in science")

Scientist and engineering statistics are usually intertwined, but they indicate that women enter the field far less than men, though this gap is narrowing. The number of science and engineering doctorates awarded to women rose from a mere 7 percent in 1970 to 34 percent in 1985 and in engineering alone the numbers of bachelor's degrees awarded to women rose from only 385 in 1975 to more than 11000 in 1985.[[53]](#cite_note-53) [*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
